c6_eng_u1_l1:
Nouns & Pronouns Catalog

Nouns are the names of people, places, things, or abstract ideas. They form the foundational subject or object of any English sentence.

  • Proper Nouns: Specific names (e.g., London, Shakespeare, Google). Always capitalized.
  • Common Nouns: General names (e.g., city, playwright, search engine).
  • Abstract Nouns: Intangible states (e.g., courage, education, beauty).

Pronouns replace nouns to avoid awkward repetitions.

Example: Sarah called Sarah's parents becomes Sarah called her parents.
c6_eng_u1_l2:
Adjectives & Adverbs Essentials

Adjectives modify or describe nouns. They provide details of color, size, origin, and quality.

Example: She read a fascinating book on English folklore.

Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. They state how, when, where, or to what extent something happened—and often end in -ly.

  • He spoke softly. (Modifies the verb 'spoke', explaining 'how')
  • The exam was extremely difficult. (Modifies the adjective 'difficult')
c6_eng_u2_l1:
Structuring a Unified Paragraph

A good paragraph functions as a small standalone argument. It possesses three major structural pillars:

  1. Topic Sentence: States the main focus of the paragraph directly.
  2. Supporting Sentences: Gives definitions, evidence, and quotes.
  3. Concluding Sentence: Synthesizes the evidence and bridges to the next paragraph.

Pro Tip: Use cohesive logical transition signals like 'Furthermore', 'Consequently', and 'In addition' to create fluid connections between statements!
c7_eng_u1_l1:
Mastering Present Tenses

The English present tense manifests in four distinct architectural states to map real-time vs. regular events:

  • Simple Present: General truths, habits, and schedules. (e.g., I study English grammar daily.)
  • Present Continuous: Ongoing actions happening right now. (e.g., You are reading this lesson.)
  • Present Perfect: Completed events linked to the present. (e.g., He has finished his exams.)
  • Present Perfect Continuous: Actions starting in past continuing now. (e.g., We have been practicing this for hours.)
c7_eng_u1_l2:
Past Tense & Irregular Formations

We use the past tense to log fully completed actions and historic facts. While regular English verbs take standard -ed endings, irregular verbs transform entirely:

Base FormSimple PastPast Participle
GoWentGone
WriteWroteWritten
RunRanRun

Ensure you select the past participle form when modeling past perfect constructions with the helper verb had.
c7_eng_u2_l1:
Sensory Details for Active Imagery

Flat description tells; sensory writing shows! Visual, auditory, tactile, olfactory, and gustatory descriptions draw the reader immediately into your story's setting:

  • Flat: It was quite cold.
  • Sensory: The sharp, icy wind bit through my wool jacket, smelling of damp cedar and winter sleet.

Use active, dynamic action verbs instead of passive static linking verbs to speed up narrative tempo!
c8_eng_u1_l1:
Converting Active Voice to Passive

In the Active Voice, the subject performs the action. In the Passive Voice, the subject receives the action.

Active: Alexander Bell invented the telephone in 1876.
Passive: The telephone was invented by Alexander Bell in 1876.

Rules for Transition: Use the appropriate form of the auxiliary verb 'to be' matching the tense of the active verb + the past participle of the main verb.
c8_eng_u1_l2:
Formal Reports: Selecting Your Voice

When drafting scientific essays or business reports, standard protocol favors the third-person passive voice to project objective, detached professionalism.

  • Avoid (Active/Personal): I surveyed forty high-school pupils last Tuesday.
  • Prefer (Passive/Objective): Forty high-school students were surveyed during the investigative research process.

This filters out personal biases and highlights research data above the researcher.
c8_eng_u2_l1:
Context Clues & Homophones Expansion

Context Clues let you guess unfamiliar words by observing surrounding context hints: antonyms, comparisons, or restatements.

Homophones are words sharing identical sounds but carrying different spelling and definitions:

  • Their: Belonging to them. (It is their guidebook.)
  • There: Specifying a location. (Place the tablet there.)
  • They're: Contraction of 'They are'. (They're studying English grammar.)
c9_eng_u1_l1:
Reporting Statement Shifts & Backshifting

When recording someone's words without exact direct quotes, we must perform tense backshifting (moving tenses one step into the past):

Direct: Henry said, "I love local poetry."
Indirect: Henry said that he loved local poetry.
Direct TenseIndirect Backshift
Simple Present ('write')Simple Past ('wrote')
Present Perfect ('have written')Past Perfect ('had written')
c9_eng_u1_l2:
Indirect Interrogatives & Imperatives

For questions or requests, remove question marks, change word order back to statements, and use connectors like if or whether:

  • Direct question: She asked, "Do you know English?"
  • Indirect statement: She asked whether I knew English.

For imperatives, convert commands into simple infinitive phrases (e.g., "Be quiet!" becomes she told us to be quiet).
c9_eng_u2_l1:
Formal Newspaper Letter Layout

Your formal layouts for board exams must strictly adhere to the professional block style left-alignment structure:

1. Sender's Full Address
2. Date (e.g., October 24, 2026)
3. Recipient (The Editor, Daily News)
4. Salutation (Dear Sir/Madam,)
5. Subject Line (e.g., SUB: Neglected public libraries in Ward 4)
6. Body (Introduction, Complaint, Resolution Proposal)
7. Closing (Yours Faithfully,
   [Your Signature])
c11_eng_u1_l1:
Subordinate & Coordinate Clauses

A clause is a group of words containing a subject and a verb:

  • Independent Clause: Can stand alone as a complete sentence because it expresses a complete thought. (e.g., "We studied all night.")
  • Subordinate (Dependent) Clause: Cannot stand alone; it starts with subordinating conjunctions (like although, because, while) or relative pronouns.
Example: Although we studied all night, we were nervous.
c11_eng_u1_l2:
Rhetorical Devices: Master Writing

Elevate your high school essays with premium rhetorical structures used by famous speechwriters:

  • Metaphor: Immediate figurative comparisons. (e.g., Education is the passport to the future.)
  • Anaphora: Repetition of a phrase/word at key beginnings. (e.g., We shall write, we shall read, we shall succeed.)
  • Parallelism: Balancing structural forms. (e.g., Reading expands the mind, writing sharpens the wit.)
c11_eng_u2_l1:
Crafting Logical Thesis Arguments

A rigorous college-level essay requires a strong Thesis Statement in the introductory section. Your thesis should not be a mere fact, but rather a debatable argument that requires active verification:

  • Weak thesis (factual): English is a global language.
  • Strong thesis (debatable): Although English serves as a vital bridge for global trade, its unchecked expansion risks diluting local literary cultures unless preservation policies are established.
